text: The surgeon inserts a Veress needle for pneumoperitoneum, performing irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests. A trocar is introduced supraumbilically, revealing a small bubble. The operating table is adjusted to a Trendelenburg position with the patient's head down, preparing for diagnostic laparoscopy.
The surgeon pushes the small bowel upward, revealing the sigmoid colon and rectum. The sigmoid colon is maneuvered towards the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the uterus, revealing no visible adnexal pathology or endometriosis. The uterus is then pushed towards the opposite shoulder.

The surgeon begins dissection of the round ligament approximately four centimeters laterally using a 37mm ligature device. The round ligament is dissected, followed by the dissection of the fallopian tube.

The surgeon identifies the major salpings and ovarian ligament. The ovarian ligament is coagulated approximately two centimeters lateral to the uterus, followed by coagulation of the major ovarian and major salpings.
The surgeon positions the uterus by pushing it towards the opposite shoulder, focusing on the broad ligament approximately 2.5 centimeters over the right side.

The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, starting with the round ligament, followed by the fallopian tube and ovarian ligament. Both sides of the adnexal structures are completed. The uterus is then pushed towards the five o'clock position, and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated.

The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the anterior leaf of the peritoneum anteriorly while the right hand pushes the ureter laterally. The uterus is positioned at six o'clock as the dissection reaches the midline of the uterovesical fold, then moves to seven o'clock as the procedure progresses to the right side.

The surgeon separates the right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament during a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The next step involves careful separation of the bladder from surrounding tissues to minimize the risk of ureteric injury.

The surgeon identifies the criss-cross pattern of vessels in the vaginal area. The bladder pillar is separated, followed by the dissection of the posterior peritoneum. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is then separated.

The surgeon works on the right side of the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, reaching the cervical part of the uterosacral ligament. The peritoneum is pushed laterally, revealing the uterine artery anteriorly and the vein posteriorly, with the ureter sandwiched between them. The structures are less skeletonized.

The surgeon separates the peritoneum over the arc and cervical part of the uterosacral ligament. Irrigation and suction are used to clean the area, enhancing the efficiency of bipolar and harmonic devices. Coagulation of the ascending uterine vessels is performed.

The surgeon irrigates the cervical area with saline to prevent tissue damage from heat. The ascending uterine artery is coagulated and then cut on the right side, revealing the lumen of the uterine artery. The open jaws of the ligature are used to coagulate the margins to stop any oozing.

The surgeon uses the open jaw of the bipolar device to ensure hemostasis on the right side, confirming no bleeding. The left side dissection is completed, and the surgeon begins the colpotomy using the tip of the harmonic scalpel.

The assistant pushes the colpotomizer into position, ensuring its size is appropriate for effective dissection. The anterior colpotomy is nearly complete, with only a small amount of vaginal epithelium tissue remaining to be dissected.

The surgeon completes the posterior colpotomy, with the uterus now positioned within the surgical field. The colpotomizer is pulled together with the uterus, facilitating its removal from the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon performs suction irrigation to clean the surgical area, ensuring there is no bleeding. The right side salpingectomy is initiated due to the presence of a small ovarian cyst. The fallopian tube is manipulated, with the surgeon pushing it into the vagina and lifting it on the left side.

The surgeon lifts the anterior fornix and uses stone holding forceps to place the tube and ovary into the vagina. A needle is then dropped through the vagina to begin closing the posterior vaginal vault, involving the uterosacral ligament.
The surgeon uses intracorporeal continuous suturing to take full thickness bites, including the epithelium, and secures the anterior loop.

The assistant lifts the bladder peritoneum to expose the vaginal plane. The surgeon approximates the edges of the vaginal fascia and epithelium at the left corner, including the uterosacral ligament, and ties a knot before cutting the suture.
